Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered this semester. I'm 29 and juggling a lot, so I tried to carve out time on weekends and a few evenings when I could really focus. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend time creating mind maps and diagrams from the lecture content. I aimed for about 19 hours a week, but the reality was that those hours were spread out unpredictably based on what else was happening in my life.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was going on. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying every evening after work, but then life would throw something at me and I'd have to cram everything into a weekend. Deadlines were the main thing that dictated when I actually sat down to study seriously. I tried to be regular, but at my age with other responsibilities, that ideal rarely matched reality.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if there was something new posted or if I had a deadline coming up. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any announcements or updates from the instructor. The discussion forums also pulled me in occasionally when I wanted to see what other students were thinking about a topic. Honestly, a lot of my logins were quick check-ins rather than deep study sessions.
Q4: There were a few weeks early on where logging in became almost automatic, like checking email in the morning. I'd made it part of my routine to watch the lecture videos over breakfast. But that habit didn't stick once things got more demanding elsewhere in my life. The mindfulness exercises I was doing helped me stay grounded, but they couldn't create more hours in the day.
Q5: The video content was by far the most useful for me because I'm such a visual learner. I watched over 160 minutes of video content and would often pause to sketch out diagrams or take visual notes. The discussion posts were also helpful because seeing how others interpreted the material gave me different perspectives. I found that peer interactions, even brief ones, helped solidify concepts in my mind.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based resources. They just didn't stick for me the way visual content did, so I'd skim them at best. Some of the supplementary materials felt redundant if I'd already watched the video on the same topic. I also delayed a lot of the practice assignments because they felt time-consuming and I kept prioritizing other things.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to the bigger picture or to the exam. Visual demonstrations, worked examples, and anything that showed concepts in action were valuable to me. If something felt like busywork or repetitive, I'd mentally check out. I needed to feel like I was actually building understanding, not just going through motions.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt like luxuries I couldn't afford time-wise. Required ones got my attention because they had consequences, but optional ones kept getting pushed to a 'someday' list that never came. I think if I'd had more bandwidth, I would have explored them, but survival mode meant prioritizing what counted toward my grade. It's frustrating because some optional content actually looked interesting.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture slides and videos, then work with them on my tablet during my commute or at a coffee shop without wifi. I also used external YouTube videos and textbooks to supplement what I was learning. My handwritten notes and visual diagrams were a big part of my study process that never showed up anywhere online.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with printed materials or my own notes but the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours working through problems on paper or creating concept maps at my kitchen table. Sometimes I'd discuss course content with a friend who works in a related field, and those conversations were incredibly valuable. None of that intellectual work registered anywhere digitally.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my preferred approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then review it multiple times offline. My tablet became my portable classroom essentially. I found I concentrated better without the distractions of being online, so I'd intentionally disconnect to study. The LMS probably showed one view when I'd actually engaged with that content five or six times.
Q12: The hardest period was mid-semester when my attendance dropped significantly and I was struggling to keep up with assignments. I was dealing with some personal stress that made it hard to focus, even though my anxiety levels stayed relatively manageable. There were weeks where I felt like I was just treading water. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope emotionally, but it couldn't solve the practical time crunch.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, my LMS use became very surface-level. I'd log in, glance at things, maybe start a video but not finish it. Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor—at 29, I have obligations that younger students might not have. There were stretches where I knew I should be engaging more deeply but just couldn't muster the energy or focus.
Q14: Oh yes, more times than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, thinking about other problems in my life. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials without really processing anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions. I'd technically logged activity, but I wasn't actually learning anything meaningful in those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important for my grade. Part of it was perfectionism—I wanted to do them well and kept waiting for the 'right' time when I'd have enough energy and focus. Part of it was avoidance because some tasks felt overwhelming when I was already stressed. My assignment completion rate suffered a lot because of this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS activity. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift toward other priorities. When something was due, suddenly I'd be logging in multiple times, watching videos, checking rubrics. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was what got me moving. The problem was that deadline-driven studying meant I was always catching up rather than staying ahead.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got encouraging feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But when feedback was delayed or minimal, I felt disconnected from whether I was even on the right track. My assignment scores were decent when I actually submitted work, so positive feedback did reinforce my efforts. I just wish I'd gotten more timely responses to keep my momentum going.
Q18: The course structure felt a bit rigid for someone in my situation. Weekly modules with set deadlines didn't account for the irregular rhythms of adult life. I appreciated having everything organized clearly, but the pacing assumed a consistency I couldn't maintain. More flexibility in when things were due would have helped me engage more steadily rather than in bursts.
Q19: The visual resources and video content made engagement easier for me—that matched my learning style perfectly. What made it harder was when important information was buried in dense text documents or when the navigation felt clunky. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged participation, which I appreciated. But the overall volume of content sometimes felt overwhelming when I was already pressed for time.
Q20: I think that could reflect someone who's genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce finished work. Maybe they're perfectionists who can't submit until something feels 'good enough.' Or they might be dealing with external pressures that eat into their assignment time. High activity might show engagement and intention, even if the output doesn't materialize.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat, though my exam score wasn't great. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show in the LMS. Someone could absolutely master material through textbooks, study groups, or other resources and only minimally touch the online platform. The LMS is just one window into learning, and for some students, it's not even the main one.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing, and which resources get clicked. They're good at capturing digital footprints. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether someone was actually thinking deeply or just scrolling. They miss offline study, emotional investment, and the understanding that happens in someone's mind. Numbers don't capture learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively processing what I'm learning, not just exposing myself to content. It means caring about understanding, asking questions, making connections to what I already know. For me, engagement involves creating something—notes, diagrams, explanations in my own words. It's an active state of mind, not just a behavior that can be logged.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I genuinely wanted to succeed in this course, despite my struggles. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials or the mental energy I invested in trying to understand difficult concepts. It wouldn't show the mindfulness practices that helped me stay resilient when things got hard, or the happiness I felt when concepts finally clicked. My engagement was real, even when my metrics didn't reflect it.
